questions.title: Single Reverse Denoising Step
questions.description: Implement a single reverse (denoising) step of a DDPM. Given the noisy input x_t, the predicted noise, and the noise schedule parameters, compute x_{t-1}.
questions.info: In the reverse process of DDPM (Ho et al. 2020), each denoising step computes x_{t-1} from x_t by removing the predicted noise. The formula is x_{t-1} = (1/sqrt(alpha_t)) * (x_t - (beta_t / sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t)) * predicted_noise) + sigma_t * z, where z ~ N(0, I). The term beta_t / sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) scales the predicted noise to match the noise level at timestep t, and sigma_t controls the stochasticity of the reverse process. This is the core computation that allows iterative refinement from pure noise to a clean sample.
questions.code:
import torch

def reverse_step(x_t, predicted_noise, t, alpha, alpha_bar, beta):
    """Single DDPM reverse step: compute x_{t-1} from x_t."""
    alpha_t = alpha[t]
    alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t]
    beta_t = beta[t]
    
    # Compute the mean of p(x_{t-1} | x_t)
    coeff1 = {{diff_adv_rs_b1}}
    coeff2 = beta_t / {{diff_adv_rs_b2}}
    mean = coeff1 * (x_t - coeff2 * predicted_noise)
    
    # Add noise (except at t=0)
    sigma_t = torch.sqrt(beta_t)
    z = torch.randn_like(x_t) if t > 0 else torch.zeros_like(x_t)
    x_prev = {{diff_adv_rs_b3}}
    return x_prev

blanks.hint: The first coefficient is 1 / sqrt(alpha_t), which rescales x_t back toward the data distribution.
The denominator in the noise coefficient is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t), the cumulative noise level.
Add scaled Gaussian noise to the computed mean: mean + sigma_t * z.

questions.title: U-Net Sinusoidal Time Embedding
questions.description: Implement the sinusoidal positional encoding used for timestep embedding in diffusion U-Nets, similar to transformer positional encodings.
questions.info: Diffusion models condition the denoising network on the current timestep t. Following the approach in DDPM (Ho et al. 2020), the timestep is encoded using sinusoidal embeddings (borrowed from the Transformer architecture). For a given timestep t and embedding dimension d, the embedding uses sin(t * freq) for the first half and cos(t * freq) for the second half, where freq = exp(-log(10000) * i / (d/2)) for position i. This provides a smooth, continuous representation of time that helps the network distinguish different noise levels.
questions.code:
import torch
import math

def sinusoidal_time_embedding(t, dim):
    """Create sinusoidal time embeddings.
    Args:
        t: tensor of timesteps, shape (batch_size,)
        dim: embedding dimension (must be even)
    Returns:
        embeddings of shape (batch_size, dim)
    """
    half_dim = dim // 2
    freqs = torch.exp(
        {{diff_adv_te_b1}} * torch.arange(half_dim, device=t.device) / half_dim
    )
    args = t[:, None] * freqs[None, :]
    embedding = torch.cat([{{diff_adv_te_b2}}], dim=-1)
    return embedding

blanks.hint: The frequency base uses -log(10000) as the scaling factor, following the original Transformer encoding.
Concatenate sin and cos of the arguments to form the full embedding of size dim.

questions.title: U-Net Residual Block with Time Conditioning
questions.description: Implement a residual block for a diffusion U-Net that incorporates the timestep embedding via addition after a projection layer.
questions.info: The U-Net architecture in DDPM uses residual blocks conditioned on the timestep. The time embedding is projected to match the feature channel dimension and then added to the hidden features between two convolution layers. This additive conditioning allows the network to modulate its behavior based on the noise level at each timestep. The architecture typically follows: Conv -> GroupNorm -> SiLU -> (add time embedding) -> Conv -> GroupNorm -> SiLU -> residual connection. GroupNorm is preferred over BatchNorm in diffusion models for stability.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ResidualBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_ch, out_ch, time_emb_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, 3, padding=1)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(out_ch, out_ch, 3, padding=1)
        self.norm1 = nn.GroupNorm(8, out_ch)
        self.norm2 = nn.GroupNorm(8, out_ch)
        self.act = nn.SiLU()
        self.time_mlp = nn.Linear(time_emb_dim, out_ch)
        self.residual_conv = nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, 1) if in_ch != out_ch else nn.Identity()

    def forward(self, x, t_emb):
        h = self.act(self.norm1(self.conv1(x)))
        # Project time embedding and add to features
        t_proj = {{diff_adv_rb_b1}}
        h = h + t_proj[:, :, {{diff_adv_rb_b2}}]
        h = self.act(self.norm2(self.conv2(h)))
        return h + {{diff_adv_rb_b3}}

blanks.hint: Project the time embedding through the linear layer (optionally with activation) to match channel dimension.
Unsqueeze the time projection to broadcast over spatial dimensions: [:, :, None, None] for (B, C, 1, 1).
Apply the residual connection, using a 1x1 conv if channel dimensions differ.

questions.title: DDPM Forward Process (q-sample)
questions.description: Implement the forward diffusion process that adds noise to clean data x_0 to get x_t in a single step using the reparameterization trick.
questions.info: The forward process in DDPM (Ho et al. 2020) is defined as q(x_t | x_0) = N(x_t; sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0, (1 - alpha_bar_t) * I). Thanks to the reparameterization trick, we can sample x_t directly as x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon, where epsilon ~ N(0, I). This closed-form expression avoids sequential noising steps during training, making it efficient to jump to any arbitrary timestep t. The alpha_bar_t is the cumulative product of (1 - beta_t) values.
questions.code:
import torch

def q_sample(x_0, t, alpha_bar, noise=None):
    """Forward diffusion: sample x_t from x_0.
    Args:
        x_0: clean data, shape (B, C, H, W)
        t: timesteps, shape (B,)
        alpha_bar: cumulative product schedule
        noise: optional pre-sampled noise
    """
    if noise is None:
        noise = torch.randn_like(x_0)
    
    sqrt_alpha_bar = {{diff_adv_fp_b1}}
    sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar = {{diff_adv_fp_b2}}
    
    # Reshape for broadcasting over (C, H, W)
    sqrt_alpha_bar = sqrt_alpha_bar[:, None, None, None]
    sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar = sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar[:, None, None, None]
    
    return {{diff_adv_fp_b3}}

blanks.hint: Extract sqrt(alpha_bar) at the given timesteps t.
Compute sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) for the noise scaling coefficient.
Reparameterization: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * noise.

questions.title: DDPM Training Loss (Noise Prediction)
questions.description: Implement the simplified DDPM training loss: sample a random timestep, noise the data, predict the noise with the model, and compute MSE loss.
questions.info: The simplified training objective in DDPM (Ho et al. 2020) is L_simple = E_{t, x_0, epsilon}[||epsilon - epsilon_theta(x_t, t)||^2]. During each training step: (1) sample a random timestep t, (2) sample noise epsilon ~ N(0, I), (3) create noisy x_t via the forward process, (4) predict the noise with the model, and (5) compute MSE between true and predicted noise. This simplified loss drops the weighting term from the variational lower bound but works better empirically. The model learns to predict the noise component at each timestep.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def ddpm_training_step(model, x_0, alpha_bar, T):
    """One DDPM training step.
    Args:
        model: noise prediction network epsilon_theta(x_t, t)
        x_0: clean data batch, shape (B, C, H, W)
        alpha_bar: cumulative alpha schedule, shape (T,)
        T: total number of timesteps
    Returns:
        loss: scalar MSE loss
    """
    B = x_0.shape[0]
    # Sample random timesteps
    t = {{diff_adv_tl_b1}}
    # Sample Gaussian noise
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_0)
    # Create noisy samples
    x_t = q_sample(x_0, t, alpha_bar, noise)
    # Predict the noise
    predicted_noise = {{diff_adv_tl_b2}}
    # Compute simplified loss
    loss = {{diff_adv_tl_b3}}
    return loss

blanks.hint: Sample random integers from 0 to T-1 for each element in the batch.
The model takes the noisy input x_t and the timestep t to predict the noise.
MSE between the true noise epsilon and the predicted noise epsilon_theta.

questions.title: DDPM Full Sampling Loop
questions.description: Implement the full DDPM sampling loop that iteratively denoises from pure Gaussian noise x_T to a clean sample x_0.
questions.info: DDPM sampling (Ho et al. 2020) starts from x_T ~ N(0, I) and iteratively applies the learned reverse process for t = T-1, T-2, ..., 0. At each step, the model predicts the noise in x_t, then computes x_{t-1} using the reverse process formula. The loop must iterate backwards through all T timesteps, which makes DDPM sampling slow compared to methods like DDIM. The use of torch.no_grad() is essential during sampling since we only need forward passes. Each step removes a small amount of noise, gradually revealing the generated sample.
questions.code:
import torch

@torch.no_grad()
def ddpm_sample(model, shape, T, alpha, alpha_bar, beta, device):
    """Generate samples using DDPM reverse process.
    Args:
        model: trained noise prediction network
        shape: shape of samples to generate (B, C, H, W)
        T: number of diffusion timesteps
    Returns:
        x_0: generated samples
    """
    # Start from pure noise
    x = {{diff_adv_sl_b1}}
    
    for t_val in {{diff_adv_sl_b2}}:
        t_batch = torch.full((shape[0],), t_val, device=device, dtype=torch.long)
        predicted_noise = model(x, t_batch)
        x = reverse_step(x, predicted_noise, t_val, alpha, alpha_bar, beta)
    
    return {{diff_adv_sl_b3}}

blanks.hint: Initialize x_T as pure Gaussian noise with the given shape on the specified device.
Iterate backwards from t = T-1 down to t = 0.
Clamp the final output to [-1, 1] for image generation (common practice).

questions.title: DDIM Deterministic Sampling Step
questions.description: Implement a single DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) sampling step, which enables deterministic generation and faster sampling.
questions.info: DDIM (Song et al. 2020) generalizes DDPM by introducing a non-Markovian reverse process. The key formula is: x_{t-1} = sqrt(alpha_bar_{t-1}) * x0_pred + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_{t-1} - sigma^2) * eps_pred + sigma * noise, where x0_pred = (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * eps_pred) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t). When sigma=0, sampling becomes fully deterministic, enabling consistent generation from the same initial noise. DDIM also allows skipping timesteps (e.g., using only 50 out of 1000 steps) for much faster generation without retraining the model.
questions.code:
import torch

def ddim_step(x_t, predicted_noise, t, t_prev, alpha_bar, eta=0.0):
    """Single DDIM sampling step.
    Args:
        x_t: noisy sample at timestep t
        predicted_noise: model's noise prediction
        t, t_prev: current and previous timestep indices
        alpha_bar: cumulative alpha schedule
        eta: controls stochasticity (0 = deterministic DDIM)
    """
    alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t]
    alpha_bar_prev = alpha_bar[t_prev] if t_prev >= 0 else torch.tensor(1.0)
    
    # Predict x_0 from x_t and predicted noise
    x0_pred = {{diff_adv_ddim_b1}}
    
    # Compute sigma for stochasticity
    sigma = eta * torch.sqrt((1 - alpha_bar_prev) / (1 - alpha_bar_t) * (1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_prev))
    
    # Direction pointing to x_t
    dir_xt = {{diff_adv_ddim_b2}}
    
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_t) if eta > 0 else torch.zeros_like(x_t)
    x_prev = {{diff_adv_ddim_b3}}
    return x_prev

blanks.hint: Predict x_0: (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * eps_pred) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t).
The direction term is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_{t-1} - sigma^2) * predicted_noise.
x_{t-1} = sqrt(alpha_bar_{t-1}) * x0_pred + direction + sigma * noise.

questions.title: DDIM Subsequence Timestep Schedule
questions.description: Create a subsequence of timesteps for accelerated DDIM sampling, selecting evenly spaced steps from the full schedule.
questions.info: One of DDIM's key advantages (Song et al. 2020) is the ability to sample using a subsequence of the original T timesteps. Instead of iterating through all 1000 steps, we can select S evenly spaced timesteps (e.g., S=50) and perform the reverse process only at those steps. This yields a 20x speedup with minimal quality loss. The subsequence is constructed by taking every (T/S)-th timestep from the original schedule. This works because the DDIM formulation directly maps between any two noise levels without requiring intermediate steps.

    """

questions.title: Classifier-Free Guidance
questions.description: Implement classifier-free guidance which combines conditional and unconditional noise predictions using a guidance scale.
questions.info: Classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022) is the dominant technique for conditional generation in modern diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2, Imagen). During training, the condition (e.g., text) is randomly dropped with some probability, so the model learns both conditional and unconditional generation. At inference, the guided prediction is: eps_guided = eps_uncond + w * (eps_cond - eps_uncond), where w is the guidance scale. Higher w values (typically 5-15) produce outputs that more strongly match the condition at the cost of diversity. This requires two forward passes per step (or a single batched pass).
questions.code:
import torch

def classifier_free_guidance_step(model, x_t, t, cond, guidance_scale):
    """Perform one denoising step with classifier-free guidance.
    Args:
        model: noise prediction model that takes (x, t, cond)
              pass cond=None for unconditional prediction
        x_t: noisy input
        t: current timestep
        cond: conditioning (e.g., text embedding)
        guidance_scale: w, strength of guidance (w=1 means no guidance)
    Returns:
        guided noise prediction
    """
    # Get conditional and unconditional predictions
    eps_cond = {{diff_adv_cfg_b1}}
    eps_uncond = {{diff_adv_cfg_b2}}
    
    # Apply guidance formula
    eps_guided = {{diff_adv_cfg_b3}}
    return eps_guided

The unconditional prediction passes None (or a null/empty embedding) as the condition.
Guided prediction = unconditional + guidance_scale * (conditional - unconditional).

questions.title: Score Function from Noise Prediction
questions.description: Derive the score function (gradient of log probability) from the noise prediction network, connecting score-based and denoising perspectives.
questions.info: Score-based generative models (Song & Ermon, 2019) learn the score function: the gradient of the log probability density, nabla_x log p(x). In the DDPM framework, the score at noise level t relates to the noise prediction as: score(x_t, t) = -epsilon_theta(x_t, t) / sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t). This connection was formalized in Song et al. (2020) 'Score-Based Generative Modeling through SDEs', unifying denoising diffusion and score matching into a single framework. The score function points toward higher-density regions of the data distribution.

    Returns:

questions.title: Langevin Dynamics Sampling
questions.description: Implement annealed Langevin dynamics for sampling from a distribution using the score function, as proposed in NCSN (Song & Ermon, 2019).
questions.info: Langevin dynamics is a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method that uses the score function to sample from a distribution. The update rule is: x_{i+1} = x_i + (step_size / 2) * score(x_i) + sqrt(step_size) * z, where z ~ N(0, I). In Noise Conditional Score Networks (NCSN, Song & Ermon 2019), annealed Langevin dynamics runs multiple steps at each noise level, starting from high noise and gradually decreasing. The step_size/2 term follows from the continuous Langevin SDE discretization. This was a precursor to the diffusion model framework.
questions.code:
import torch

def langevin_dynamics_step(x, score_fn, step_size):
    """One step of Langevin dynamics.
    x_{i+1} = x_i + (step_size/2) * score(x_i) + sqrt(step_size) * z
    Args:
        x: current sample
        score_fn: function that returns nabla_x log p(x)
        step_size: Langevin step size (epsilon)
    Returns:
        x_next: updated sample
    """
    z = torch.randn_like(x)
    score = score_fn(x)
    x_next = {{diff_adv_ld_b1}}
    return x_next

def annealed_langevin(score_model, shape, sigmas, n_steps_each, step_size, device):
    """Annealed Langevin dynamics across noise levels."""
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)
    for sigma in sigmas:
        for _ in range(n_steps_each):
            score = score_model(x, sigma)
            z = torch.randn_like(x)
            step = {{diff_adv_ld_b2}} * (sigma ** 2)
            x = x + (step / 2) * score + {{diff_adv_ld_b3}} * z
    return x

blanks.hint: Langevin update: x + (step_size/2) * score + sqrt(step_size) * z.
The annealed step size is scaled by sigma^2; the base step_size multiplied by sigma^2 outside.
The noise coefficient is sqrt(step), the square root of the annealed step size.

questions.title: Conditional Generation with Dynamic Guidance
questions.description: Implement a sampling function that supports dynamic guidance scale scheduling, where guidance strength varies during the sampling process.
questions.info: In practice, the classifier-free guidance scale does not need to be constant throughout sampling. Research has shown that using higher guidance in later (less noisy) steps and lower guidance in early (more noisy) steps can improve sample quality. The guidance scale can follow a linear schedule from w_min to w_max across the sampling trajectory. Some works (e.g., eDiff-I from NVIDIA) also use different guidance for different components. Dynamic guidance helps balance diversity (lower guidance) in global structure and fidelity (higher guidance) in fine details.

blanks.hint: Progress goes from 0 (at t=T) to 1 (at t=0). Compute as 1 - t/T.
Call the guidance scale function with the current timestep and total timesteps.
Apply classifier-free guidance: eps_uncond + w * (eps_cond - eps_uncond).

questions.title: EMA (Exponential Moving Average) for Model Weights
questions.description: Implement an EMA class that maintains a shadow copy of model parameters, commonly used in diffusion model training for stable sampling.
questions.info: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of model weights is a critical technique in diffusion model training (used in DDPM, Stable Diffusion, etc.). The EMA parameters are updated as: ema_param = decay * ema_param + (1 - decay) * model_param. Typical decay values are 0.999 or 0.9999. The EMA model is used only for sampling/evaluation, not training. It produces smoother, higher-quality samples by averaging out training noise. The technique was popularized in GANs and is now standard in diffusion models. The copy_to method allows swapping EMA weights into the model for inference.

blanks.hint: EMA update: decay * old_ema + (1 - decay) * new_param. Blend old shadow with current param.
Load the shadow (EMA) parameters into the model using load_state_dict.
Deep copy the model's current state_dict as a backup before swapping in EMA weights.

questions.title: Linear and Cosine Noise Schedules
questions.description: Implement both the linear beta schedule (from DDPM) and the cosine schedule (from Improved DDPM) for the noise variance.
questions.info: The noise schedule defines how much noise is added at each timestep and is crucial for diffusion model performance. The original DDPM (Ho et al. 2020) uses a linear schedule: beta_t linearly interpolates from beta_start to beta_end. Improved DDPM (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) introduced the cosine schedule, which provides a more gradual noise increase, preventing the image from being destroyed too quickly. The cosine schedule defines alpha_bar_t = cos((t/T + s) / (1 + s) * pi/2)^2 where s=0.008 is a small offset. The cosine schedule generally produces better results, especially for high-resolution images.

blanks.hint: Use torch.linspace to create T evenly spaced values from beta_start to beta_end.
f(t) = cos((t/T + s) / (1 + s) * pi/2)^2. Use math.pi for pi.
Clamp betas to a maximum of 0.999 to prevent numerical issues at the end of the schedule.

questions.title: V-Prediction Parameterization
questions.description: Implement the v-prediction parameterization (Salimans & Ho, 2022) as an alternative to noise prediction, and show how to convert between v, epsilon, and x_0.
questions.info: V-prediction (Salimans & Ho, 2022 - 'Progressive Distillation for Fast Sampling of Diffusion Models') parameterizes the model to predict v = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * epsilon - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * x_0, instead of predicting epsilon or x_0 directly. This parameterization has been shown to be more numerically stable, especially at high noise levels, and enables progressive distillation for faster sampling. From v, we can recover both x_0 and epsilon. Stable Diffusion v2 and many modern models use v-prediction for improved training stability.

blanks.hint: v = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * epsilon - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * x_0.
x_0 = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * v.
eps = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * v + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * x_t.

questions.title: U-Net Encoder (Down) Block
questions.description: Implement a down-sampling block for the diffusion U-Net encoder with residual blocks and spatial downsampling.
questions.info: The U-Net architecture used in diffusion models (Ronneberger et al., 2015; adapted by Ho et al., 2020) consists of an encoder (down) path and decoder (up) path with skip connections. Each encoder block typically contains residual convolution blocks followed by spatial downsampling (usually 2x2 stride-2 convolution or average pooling). The encoder progressively reduces spatial resolution while increasing channel depth, building a hierarchy of features. Skip connections from encoder to decoder are crucial for preserving spatial details during the denoising process.

blanks.hint: Use a strided convolution for downsampling: kernel_size=3, stride=2, padding=1 halves spatial dims.
Pass both the feature map x and the time embedding t_emb to each residual block.
Apply the downsampling layer to reduce spatial resolution by 2x.

questions.title: U-Net Decoder (Up) Block with Skip Connections
questions.description: Implement an up-sampling block for the diffusion U-Net decoder that incorporates skip connections from the encoder.
questions.info: The decoder (up) path of the U-Net first upsamples the spatial dimensions, then concatenates skip connections from the corresponding encoder level along the channel dimension. This channel concatenation is followed by residual blocks that process the combined features. Skip connections are essential because they provide fine-grained spatial information that was lost during encoding. In diffusion U-Nets, upsampling is typically done via nearest-neighbor interpolation followed by a convolution (rather than transposed convolution) to avoid checkerboard artifacts.

questions.title: Self-Attention Layer in Diffusion U-Net
questions.description: Implement a self-attention layer for the diffusion U-Net, typically applied at lower spatial resolutions (e.g., 16x16 or 8x8).
questions.info: Self-attention layers are incorporated at specific resolution levels in diffusion U-Nets (typically 16x16 or 8x8) to capture long-range dependencies. As noted in DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) and ADM (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021), attention at lower resolutions significantly improves sample quality without prohibitive computational cost. The spatial feature map is reshaped to a sequence, multi-head self-attention is applied, and the result is reshaped back. GroupNorm is applied before attention (pre-norm), and a residual connection preserves the original features.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SpatialSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, channels, num_heads=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.channels = channels
        self.norm = nn.GroupNorm(8, channels)
        self.qkv = nn.Conv1d(channels, channels * 3, 1)
        self.proj_out = nn.Conv1d(channels, channels, 1)
        self.num_heads = num_heads

    def forward(self, x):
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        h = self.norm(x)
        # Reshape spatial dims to sequence
        h = {{diff_adv_sa_b1}}
        qkv = self.qkv(h).chunk(3, dim=1)
        q, k, v = qkv
        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scale = {{diff_adv_sa_b2}}
        attn = torch.bmm(q.transpose(1, 2), k) * scale
        attn = torch.softmax(attn, dim=-1)
        h = torch.bmm(v, attn.transpose(1, 2))
        h = self.proj_out(h)
        # Reshape back and residual
        return {{diff_adv_sa_b3}}

blanks.hint: Flatten the spatial dimensions (H, W) into a single sequence dimension: (B, C, H*W).
The attention scaling factor is 1/sqrt(d_k) where d_k = C / num_heads.
Reshape back to (B, C, H, W) and add the residual connection with the original input x.

questions.title: Noise Prediction vs x0 Prediction Loss
questions.description: Implement training loss computation for both noise-prediction and x0-prediction parameterizations, showing they are equivalent up to a weighting factor.
questions.info: Diffusion models can be parameterized to predict either the noise epsilon (DDPM default) or the clean data x_0. With noise prediction, the loss is ||epsilon - epsilon_theta(x_t, t)||^2. With x_0 prediction, the loss is ||x_0 - x0_theta(x_t, t)||^2. These are mathematically equivalent up to a timestep-dependent weighting: predicting x_0 from the noise prediction is x0_pred = (x_t - sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*eps_pred) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t). In practice, noise prediction works better for generation quality, while x_0 prediction can be better for certain tasks like inpainting. The choice of parameterization affects the implicit loss weighting across timesteps.

blanks.hint: MSE between predicted noise and true noise.
MSE between predicted clean image and true clean image.
x_0 = (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * eps) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t) from the forward process formula.

questions.title: Classifier-Free Guidance Training with Random Dropout
questions.description: Implement the training procedure for classifier-free guidance where the conditioning signal is randomly dropped with a fixed probability.
questions.info: Classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022) requires a specific training procedure: during training, the conditioning signal (e.g., class label or text embedding) is randomly replaced with a null/unconditional token with probability p_uncond (typically 0.1 to 0.2). This teaches the model both conditional and unconditional generation simultaneously in a single network. At inference time, both predictions are available without needing a separate classifier. The null condition is often represented as a zero vector or a learned unconditional embedding. This dropout-based training is simple yet remarkably effective.

questions.title: Consistency Model Direct Mapping
questions.description: Implement the core consistency function that maps any noisy sample x_t directly to the clean data x_0 in a single step, as proposed in Consistency Models (Song et al., 2023).
questions.info: Consistency Models (Song et al., 2023) learn a function f(x_t, t) that maps any point along the probability flow ODE trajectory directly to the trajectory's origin x_0. The key consistency property is that f(x_t, t) = f(x_t', t') for any two points on the same trajectory. At the boundary condition t=epsilon (near zero), the model should output x_t itself (identity). This is enforced by parameterizing f(x, t) = c_skip(t) * x + c_out(t) * F_theta(x, t), where c_skip(epsilon)=1 and c_out(epsilon)=0. This allows single-step generation, making sampling orders of magnitude faster than iterative diffusion.

blanks.hint: c_skip(sigma) = sigma_data^2 / (sigma^2 + sigma_data^2). This equals 1 when sigma is very small.
c_out(sigma) = sigma * sigma_data / sqrt(sigma^2 + sigma_data^2). This equals 0 when sigma is very small.
The output is c_skip * x + c_out * F_theta(x, sigma), enforcing the boundary condition at sigma_min.

questions.title: Consistency Distillation Loss
questions.description: Implement the consistency distillation loss that trains a consistency model from a pre-trained diffusion model by enforcing consistent predictions along the ODE trajectory.
questions.info: Consistency distillation trains a student consistency model f_theta from a teacher diffusion model. For adjacent timesteps t_{n+1} and t_n, the teacher ODE solver produces x_hat from x at t_{n+1}, and the loss enforces f_theta(x, t_{n+1}) = f_theta_ema(x_hat, t_n). The target network uses EMA parameters (stop-gradient) to stabilize training, similar to momentum-based methods in self-supervised learning. The distance metric d() is typically the Pseudo-Huber loss or LPIPS for images. This distillation approach leverages a pre-trained diffusion model to avoid the difficulty of training consistency models from scratch.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def consistency_distillation_loss(model, model_ema, x_0, noise, t_n, t_n1, alpha_bar, ode_solver_fn):
    """Consistency distillation loss.
    Args:
        model: student consistency model f_theta
        model_ema: EMA target consistency model f_theta_ema (no grad)
        x_0: clean data
        noise: sampled noise
        t_n, t_n1: adjacent timestep indices (t_n < t_n1)
        alpha_bar: noise schedule
        ode_solver_fn: one step of the teacher ODE solver
    """
    # Create noisy sample at t_{n+1}
    x_tn1 = q_sample(x_0, t_n1, alpha_bar, noise)
    
    # Use teacher ODE solver to estimate x at t_n
    with torch.no_grad():
        x_hat_tn = {{diff_adv_new_cd_b1}}
    
    # Student prediction at t_{n+1}
    pred_student = {{diff_adv_new_cd_b2}}
    
    # Target prediction at t_n (stop gradient via EMA model)
    with torch.no_grad():
        pred_target = {{diff_adv_new_cd_b3}}
    
    # Pseudo-Huber loss as distance metric
    c = 0.00054 * (x_0.shape[-1] * x_0.shape[-2]) ** 0.5
    loss = torch.sqrt((pred_student - pred_target) ** 2 + c ** 2) - c
    return loss.mean()

The student model predicts from the noisy sample x at timestep t_{n+1}.
The EMA target model predicts from the ODE-solved sample at timestep t_n.

questions.title: VP-SDE Forward Process (Variance Preserving SDE)
questions.description: Implement the forward noising process of the Variance Preserving SDE (VP-SDE), which generalizes the DDPM forward process to continuous time.
questions.info: The VP-SDE (Song et al., 2020 'Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations') is the continuous-time generalization of DDPM. The forward SDE is dx = -0.5 * beta(t) * x dt + sqrt(beta(t)) dw, where beta(t) is a continuous noise schedule. The transition kernel is q(x_t | x_0) = N(x_t; x_0 * exp(-0.5 * integral_0^t beta(s) ds), I - I * exp(-integral_0^t beta(s) ds)). For a linear schedule beta(t) = beta_min + t*(beta_max - beta_min), the integral has a closed form. This SDE framework unifies DDPM, SMLD, and other diffusion variants.
questions.code:
import torch

def vp_sde_marginal_params(t, beta_min=0.1, beta_max=20.0):
    """Compute mean coefficient and std for VP-SDE at continuous time t in [0, 1].
    Args:
        t: continuous time in [0, 1], shape (B,) or scalar
        beta_min, beta_max: linear schedule endpoints
    Returns:
        mean_coeff: coefficient for x_0 in the mean
        std: standard deviation of the noise
    """
    # Integral of beta(s) from 0 to t for linear schedule
    log_mean_coeff = {{diff_adv_new_vpsde_b1}}
    mean_coeff = torch.exp(log_mean_coeff)
    std = {{diff_adv_new_vpsde_b2}}
    return mean_coeff, std

def vp_sde_sample(x_0, t, beta_min=0.1, beta_max=20.0):
    """Sample x_t from the VP-SDE forward process."""
    mean_coeff, std = vp_sde_marginal_params(t, beta_min, beta_max)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_0)
    # Reshape for broadcasting
    mean_coeff = mean_coeff.view(-1, *([1] * (x_0.dim() - 1)))
    std = std.view(-1, *([1] * (x_0.dim() - 1)))
    return {{diff_adv_new_vpsde_b3}}

blanks.hint: The integral of beta(s)/2 from 0 to t with linear schedule is 0.5*(beta_min*t + 0.5*(beta_max-beta_min)*t^2). The negative gives the log mean coefficient.
The variance is 1 - exp(2 * log_mean_coeff) = 1 - mean_coeff^2. Take its square root for std.
Reparameterization trick: x_t = mean_coeff * x_0 + std * noise.

questions.title: VE-SDE Forward Process (Variance Exploding SDE)
questions.description: Implement the forward process of the Variance Exploding SDE (VE-SDE), which generalizes the NCSN/SMLD noise perturbation to continuous time.
questions.info: The VE-SDE (Song et al., 2020) is defined as dx = sqrt(d[sigma^2(t)]/dt) dw, which simply adds noise without scaling the signal. The transition kernel is q(x_t | x_0) = N(x_t; x_0, sigma(t)^2 * I), meaning the mean is always x_0 and the variance grows over time. A common sigma schedule is geometric: sigma(t) = sigma_min * (sigma_max/sigma_min)^t for t in [0,1]. Unlike VP-SDE, the signal is not attenuated, only the noise increases. This makes VE-SDE simpler conceptually but the variance 'explodes' to very large values.

blanks.hint: The geometric schedule interpolates exponentially: sigma_min * (sigma_max/sigma_min)^t.
In VE-SDE, the mean is x_0 (no signal attenuation). Just add sigma-scaled noise.
For a Gaussian q(x_t|x_0) = N(x_0, sigma^2 I), the score is -(x_t - x_0) / sigma^2.

questions.title: Probability Flow ODE Step
questions.description: Implement a single Euler step of the probability flow ODE, which enables deterministic sampling from the SDE framework without stochasticity.
questions.info: For every diffusion SDE, there exists a corresponding deterministic ODE (the probability flow ODE) that has the same marginal distributions at each time t. For the VP-SDE, the probability flow ODE is dx/dt = -0.5 * beta(t) * [x + score(x,t)]. This ODE can be solved with standard ODE solvers (Euler, Heun, RK45, etc.) to generate samples deterministically. The probability flow ODE is central to DDIM, exact likelihood computation via the change-of-variables formula, and consistency models. Deterministic sampling also enables meaningful interpolation in latent space.

    dx/dt = -0.5 * beta(t) * [x + score(x, t)]
    Args:
        x: current state
        t: current time (scalar)
        dt: time step (negative for reverse/generation)
        score_fn: score model score(x, t)
        beta_fn: function returning beta(t)
    Returns:
        x_next: state at t + dt
    """
    beta_t = beta_fn(t)
    score = score_fn(x, t)
    # Drift of the probability flow ODE
    drift = {{diff_adv_new_pfode_b1}}
    # Euler step
    x_next = {{diff_adv_new_pfode_b2}}
    return x_next

def ode_sample_loop(score_fn, beta_fn, shape, device, N=1000, t_start=1.0, t_end=0.001):
    """Full ODE sampling from t_start (noisy) to t_end (clean)."""
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)
    dt = {{diff_adv_new_pfode_b3}}

questions.title: Predictor-Corrector Sampler
questions.description: Implement the predictor-corrector sampling scheme that alternates between a reverse-SDE predictor step and a Langevin corrector step for improved sample quality.
questions.info: The predictor-corrector (PC) sampler (Song et al., 2020) combines two complementary operations at each timestep. The predictor step takes a numerical step of the reverse-time SDE (e.g., Euler-Maruyama), moving from noise level t to t-dt. The corrector step then applies Langevin MCMC at the current noise level to refine the sample toward the correct marginal distribution. This two-phase approach reduces discretization errors from the predictor by correcting them with score-based MCMC. Multiple corrector steps can be applied per predictor step for higher quality at the cost of more compute.
questions.code:
import torch

def predictor_step_reverse_sde(x, t, dt, score_fn, beta_fn):
    """Euler-Maruyama step of the reverse-time VP-SDE.
    dx = [-0.5*beta(t)*x - beta(t)*score(x,t)] dt + sqrt(beta(t)) dw_bar
    """
    beta_t = beta_fn(t)
    score = score_fn(x, t)
    drift = {{diff_adv_new_pc_b1}}
    diffusion = torch.sqrt(torch.tensor(beta_t))
    z = torch.randn_like(x)
    x_next = x + drift * dt + diffusion * torch.sqrt(torch.abs(torch.tensor(dt))) * z
    return x_next

def corrector_step_langevin(x, t, score_fn, snr=0.16):
    """Langevin MCMC corrector step at noise level t."""
    score = score_fn(x, t)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x)
    grad_norm = torch.mean(score ** 2)
    noise_norm = torch.mean(noise ** 2)
    step_size = {{diff_adv_new_pc_b2}}
    x_next = {{diff_adv_new_pc_b3}}
    return x_next

blanks.hint: The reverse-SDE drift is -0.5*beta(t)*x - beta(t)*score(x,t). The score term has coefficient beta(t).
The step size is 2 * snr * ||noise||^2 / ||score||^2, calibrating the step to the score magnitude.

questions.title: DPM-Solver First-Order Step
questions.description: Implement a single first-order step of DPM-Solver, a fast ODE solver specialized for diffusion models that operates in the log-SNR (lambda) space.
questions.info: DPM-Solver (Lu et al., 2022) is a high-order ODE solver designed specifically for the diffusion probability flow ODE. It reparameterizes the ODE in terms of lambda_t = log(alpha_t / sigma_t), the log signal-to-noise ratio. The first-order DPM-Solver computes x_t = (sigma_t / sigma_s) * x_s + alpha_t * (1 - exp(-(lambda_t - lambda_s))) * x0_pred_s, where x0_pred_s is the x_0 estimate at time s. This parameterization reduces approximation error compared to naive Euler steps. DPM-Solver achieves high-quality samples in 10-20 steps, compared to 50-1000 for DDPM.
questions.code:
import torch

def dpm_solver_first_order_step(x_s, s, t, model, alpha, sigma):
    """First-order DPM-Solver step from time s to time t.
    Args:
        x_s: sample at time s
        s, t: source and target time indices
        model: noise prediction model
        alpha: signal coefficients schedule
        sigma: noise coefficients schedule
    Returns:
        x_t: estimated sample at time t
    """
    # Compute log-SNR values
    lambda_s = torch.log(alpha[s] / sigma[s])
    lambda_t = torch.log(alpha[t] / sigma[t])
    h = lambda_t - lambda_s  # step size in log-SNR space
    
    # Predict x_0 from noise prediction
    eps_s = model(x_s, s)
    x0_pred = {{diff_adv_new_dpm_b1}}
    
    # DPM-Solver-1 update
    coeff_x = {{diff_adv_new_dpm_b2}}
    coeff_x0 = alpha[t] * (1.0 - torch.exp({{diff_adv_new_dpm_b3}}))
    x_t = coeff_x * x_s + coeff_x0 * x0_pred
    return x_t

blanks.hint: Predict x_0: x0 = (x_s - sigma_s * eps_pred) / alpha_s, from the forward process formula.
The coefficient for x_s is sigma_t / sigma_s, the ratio of noise levels.
The exponential term uses -h where h = lambda_t - lambda_s is the log-SNR step.

questions.title: DPM-Solver++ Second-Order Multistep
questions.description: Implement the second-order multistep variant of DPM-Solver++, which uses the data-prediction formulation and two previous estimates for higher accuracy.
questions.info: DPM-Solver++ (Lu et al., 2022b) improves upon DPM-Solver by working in the data-prediction (x_0-prediction) space rather than the noise-prediction space, which is more stable for guided sampling. The second-order multistep version uses the current and one previous x_0 estimate to perform a second-order Taylor expansion. The update is: x_t = (sigma_t/sigma_s)*x_s + alpha_t*(1-e^{-h})*D_0 + 0.5*alpha_t*(1-e^{-h})*(r/(1+r))*D_1, where D_0 and D_1 are the first and second-order data-prediction differences. This achieves excellent quality in just 15-20 steps.
questions.code:
import torch

def dpm_solver_pp_2m_step(x_s, s, t, model, alpha, sigma, x0_prev=None, s_prev=None):
    """DPM-Solver++(2M) second-order multistep update.
    Args:
        x_s: sample at time s
        s, t: source and target time indices
        model: noise prediction model
        alpha, sigma: schedules
        x0_prev: previous x_0 prediction (for second order)
        s_prev: previous timestep (for computing r)
    """
    lambda_s = torch.log(alpha[s] / sigma[s])
    lambda_t = torch.log(alpha[t] / sigma[t])
    h = lambda_t - lambda_s
    
    # Current x_0 prediction
    eps = model(x_s, s)
    x0_cur = (x_s - sigma[s] * eps) / alpha[s]
    
    if x0_prev is None:
        # First-order fallback
        x_t = (sigma[t] / sigma[s]) * x_s + alpha[t] * (1 - torch.exp(-h)) * x0_cur
    else:
        lambda_prev = torch.log(alpha[s_prev] / sigma[s_prev])
        r = {{diff_adv_new_dpmpp_b1}}
        D0 = x0_cur
        D1 = {{diff_adv_new_dpmpp_b2}}
        x_t = (sigma[t] / sigma[s]) * x_s + alpha[t] * (1 - torch.exp(-h)) * D0 + {{diff_adv_new_dpmpp_b3}}
    
    return x_t, x0_cur

blanks.hint: The ratio r = (lambda_s - lambda_{s-1}) / h controls the weighting of the second-order correction.
D1 is the finite difference of x_0 predictions: (x0_cur - x0_prev) / (2*r).
The second-order correction is 0.5 * alpha_t * (1 - e^{-h}) * 2r * D1.

questions.title: Denoising Score Matching Loss
questions.description: Implement the denoising score matching (DSM) objective that trains a score network by matching the score of a noise-perturbed distribution.
questions.info: Denoising score matching (Vincent, 2011) is a practical way to train score networks without computing the intractable score of the data distribution. Instead of matching nabla_x log p(x), we match nabla_x log q(x_tilde | x) where x_tilde = x + sigma*noise. For a Gaussian perturbation kernel, the target score is simply -(x_tilde - x) / sigma^2 = -noise / sigma. The DSM objective is E[||s_theta(x_tilde, sigma) + noise/sigma||^2]. This is equivalent to the DDPM noise prediction loss up to a scaling factor, connecting classical score matching to modern diffusion models.

questions.title: Sliced Score Matching Loss
questions.description: Implement sliced score matching, which avoids computing the Jacobian trace by projecting the score onto random directions.
questions.info: Sliced score matching (Song et al., 2019) is a scalable alternative to the original score matching objective, which requires computing tr(nabla_x s_theta(x)), a costly Jacobian trace. Instead, SSM projects the score onto random vectors v and matches the projected score. The loss is E_v[v^T nabla_x s_theta(x) v + 0.5 * (v^T s_theta(x))^2], where v is drawn from a simple distribution (e.g., Rademacher or standard Gaussian). The first term requires only a vector-Jacobian product (efficiently computed via autograd), not the full Jacobian. This makes training feasible for high-dimensional data.
questions.code:
import torch

def sliced_score_matching_loss(score_model, x):
    """Sliced score matching loss.
    Args:
        score_model: score network s_theta(x)
        x: data samples, shape (B, D)
    Returns:
        loss: scalar SSM loss
    """
    x = x.requires_grad_(True)
    score = score_model(x)
    
    # Random projection vectors (Rademacher distribution)
    v = {{diff_adv_new_ssm_b1}}
    
    # Compute v^T * nabla_x * (s_theta(x)^T v) via autograd
    sv = torch.sum(score * v)
    grad_sv = {{diff_adv_new_ssm_b2}}
    vJv = torch.sum(grad_sv * v, dim=-1)
    
    # Squared norm term: 0.5 * (v^T s_theta)^2
    sv_per_sample = torch.sum(score * v, dim=-1)
    loss = {{diff_adv_new_ssm_b3}}
    return loss

The SSM loss is E[v^T J v + 0.5 * (v^T s)^2], averaged over the batch.

questions.title: Noise Conditional Score Network (NCSN) Multi-Scale Loss
questions.description: Implement the multi-scale denoising score matching loss used in NCSN, which trains across a geometric sequence of noise levels.
questions.info: NCSN (Song & Ermon, 2019) trains a single score network conditioned on the noise level sigma. The model is trained across L noise levels forming a geometric sequence from sigma_1 (largest) to sigma_L (smallest). At each noise level, the denoising score matching loss is weighted by sigma^2 to ensure that the loss magnitudes are comparable across scales. The total loss is (1/L) * sum_{i=1}^{L} sigma_i^2 * E[||s_theta(x_tilde, sigma_i) + noise/sigma_i||^2]. This multi-scale training is critical for the model to learn scores at all noise levels for effective annealed Langevin sampling.
questions.code:
import torch

def ncsn_multi_scale_loss(score_model, x, sigmas):
    """NCSN loss across multiple noise levels.
    Args:
        score_model: score network s_theta(x, sigma)
        x: clean data, shape (B, ...)
        sigmas: tensor of noise levels, shape (L,)
    Returns:
        loss: weighted average loss across all noise levels
    """
    L = sigmas.shape[0]
    total_loss = 0.0
    
    for i in range(L):
        sigma = sigmas[i]
        noise = torch.randn_like(x)
        x_noisy = x + sigma * noise
        score_pred = score_model(x_noisy, sigma)
        target = {{diff_adv_new_ncsn_b1}}
        loss_i = {{diff_adv_new_ncsn_b2}} * torch.mean((score_pred - target) ** 2)
        total_loss = total_loss + loss_i
    
    return {{diff_adv_new_ncsn_b3}}

blanks.hint: The target score for Gaussian perturbation is -noise / sigma (gradient of log N(x, sigma^2 I)).
Weight each noise level's loss by sigma^2 to equalize the loss magnitudes across scales.
Average the total loss across all L noise levels.

questions.title: Flow Matching (Conditional Flow Matching) Loss
questions.description: Implement the conditional flow matching loss (Lipman et al., 2023) which trains a velocity field that transports noise to data along straight paths.
questions.info: Flow matching (Lipman et al., 2023) and its conditional variant train a neural ODE velocity field v_theta(x_t, t) by regressing onto conditional vector fields. The optimal transport conditional path is x_t = (1-t)*noise + t*x_0, making the target velocity simply x_0 - noise. The loss is E_{t,x_0,noise}[||v_theta(x_t, t) - (x_0 - noise)||^2]. This is simpler and more stable than score matching, as the target is a constant vector for each pair (x_0, noise). Flow matching connects to rectified flows and is the basis for Stable Diffusion 3's architecture. It avoids the need for a noise schedule entirely.
questions.code:

    x_t = {{diff_adv_new_fm_b1}}

    target_v = {{diff_adv_new_fm_b2}}
    
    # Predict velocity

questions.title: Diffusion Inpainting with RePaint
questions.description: Implement the core inpainting step from RePaint (Lugmayr et al., 2022), which uses a pre-trained diffusion model for inpainting by replacing known regions at each reverse step.
questions.info: RePaint (Lugmayr et al., 2022) performs image inpainting using a pre-trained unconditional diffusion model without any fine-tuning. At each reverse step, the known (unmasked) pixels are replaced with forward-diffused versions of the original image at the current noise level, while the unknown (masked) pixels are denoised by the model. The key formula is: x_{t-1} = mask * q_sample(x_known, t-1) + (1-mask) * model_denoised. RePaint also introduces resampling: jumping back and forth in the diffusion process to improve coherence between known and unknown regions.
questions.code:
import torch

def repaint_inpaint_step(x_t, t, model, x_known, mask, alpha_bar, alpha, beta):
    """Single RePaint inpainting step.
    Args:
        x_t: current noisy image at timestep t
        t: current timestep (integer)
        model: pre-trained noise prediction model
        x_known: original image (known content)
        mask: binary mask (1 = known/keep, 0 = unknown/inpaint)
        alpha_bar: cumulative alpha schedule
    Returns:
        x_prev: image at timestep t-1
    """
    # Denoise the full image with the model
    pred_noise = model(x_t, t)
    x_denoised_prev = reverse_step(x_t, pred_noise, t, alpha, alpha_bar, beta)
    
    # Forward-diffuse the known image to timestep t-1
    if t > 0:
        noise = torch.randn_like(x_known)
        t_prev = t - 1
        x_known_noisy = {{diff_adv_new_inp_b1}}
    else:
        x_known_noisy = x_known
    
    # Combine: keep known regions, use denoised for unknown
    x_prev = {{diff_adv_new_inp_b2}}
    return x_prev

def repaint_resample(x_prev, t, alpha_bar, beta):
    """Resample step: go forward one step to re-harmonize."""
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_prev)
    beta_t = beta[t]
    return {{diff_adv_new_inp_b3}}

The U-Net then takes this concatenated input (2*C channels) and predicts the noise. During training, the model learns to denoise while being guided by the low-res conditioning. This approach naturally handles uncertainty in the upsampling task, generating diverse plausible high-res outputs from a single low-res input.

The text prompt is tokenized, passed through the CLIP text encoder to produce a sequence of token embeddings (not just the [CLS] token), and then projected to match the U-Net's cross-attention dimension. The U-Net uses cross-attention layers where image features are queries and text embeddings are keys/values. This allows the model to attend to relevant parts of the text description at each spatial location. CLIP's joint vision-language space provides rich semantic guidance.

questions.title: ControlNet Zero Convolution and Feature Injection
questions.description: Implement the key components of ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023): the zero-initialized convolution layers and the feature injection mechanism into a frozen diffusion U-Net.
questions.info: ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023) adds spatial conditioning (edge maps, depth, pose, etc.) to a pre-trained diffusion model by creating a trainable copy of the encoder blocks. The outputs of the trainable copy are added to the frozen model's skip connections via 'zero convolutions' -- 1x1 convolutions initialized to zero weights and zero bias. This zero initialization ensures that at the start of training the ControlNet has no effect on the pre-trained model, preserving its quality. During training, only the ControlNet copy and zero convolutions are updated while the original U-Net remains frozen.

questions.title: IP-Adapter Image Prompt Conditioning
questions.description: Implement the IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023) mechanism that enables image-prompt conditioning by injecting CLIP image embeddings through a separate cross-attention path.
questions.info: IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023) enables image-conditioned generation by adding a decoupled cross-attention mechanism. The CLIP image embedding of a reference image is projected through a learned linear layer and used as keys/values in a new cross-attention layer, separate from the text cross-attention. The outputs of both attention paths are combined with a weighting factor. Only the image projection and new cross-attention layers are trained; the original model is frozen. This decoupled design prevents catastrophic forgetting and allows flexible combination of text and image prompts.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class IPAdapterCrossAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, clip_image_dim=1024, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = dim // num_heads
        # Original text cross-attention (frozen)
        self.to_q = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.to_k_text = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.to_v_text = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        # New image cross-attention (trainable)
        self.to_k_image = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.to_v_image = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.image_proj = {{diff_adv_new_ipa_b1}}
        self.to_out = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
    
    def forward(self, x, text_context, image_embedding, ip_scale=1.0):
        """x: (B, HW, D), text_context: (B, S, D), image_embedding: (B, 1, clip_dim)"""
        q = self.to_q(x)
        scale = self.head_dim ** -0.5
        
        # Text cross-attention
        k_text, v_text = self.to_k_text(text_context), self.to_v_text(text_context)
        attn_text = torch.softmax(torch.bmm(q, k_text.transpose(-1, -2)) * scale, dim=-1)
        out_text = torch.bmm(attn_text, v_text)
        
        # Image cross-attention
        image_tokens = {{diff_adv_new_ipa_b2}}
        k_img, v_img = self.to_k_image(image_tokens), self.to_v_image(image_tokens)
        attn_img = torch.softmax(torch.bmm(q, k_img.transpose(-1, -2)) * scale, dim=-1)
        out_img = torch.bmm(attn_img, v_img)
        
        # Combine text and image attention
        out = {{diff_adv_new_ipa_b3}}
        return x + self.to_out(out)

questions.title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for Diffusion Models
questions.description: Implement a LoRA layer that adds a low-rank update to a pre-trained linear layer in a diffusion U-Net, enabling efficient fine-tuning.
questions.info: LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) adapts pre-trained models by adding trainable low-rank decomposition matrices to frozen weight matrices. For a weight matrix W of shape (out, in), LoRA adds delta_W = B @ A where A has shape (rank, in) and B has shape (out, rank), with rank << min(in, out). The forward pass becomes y = Wx + (B @ A) * scale * x. A is initialized with Gaussian values and B with zeros, so the initial delta_W is zero. For diffusion models, LoRA is typically applied to the attention projection layers (Q, K, V, O) in the U-Net, reducing trainable parameters from billions to a few megabytes.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, original_linear, rank=4, alpha=1.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.original = original_linear
        self.original.weight.requires_grad_(False)
        if self.original.bias is not None:
            self.original.bias.requires_grad_(False)
        
        in_features = original_linear.in_features
        out_features = original_linear.out_features
        self.rank = rank
        self.scale = alpha / rank
        
        # Low-rank matrices
        self.lora_A = {{diff_adv_new_lora_b1}}
        self.lora_B = {{diff_adv_new_lora_b2}}
        nn.init.kaiming_uniform_(self.lora_A, a=math.sqrt(5))
        nn.init.zeros_(self.lora_B)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        # Original frozen output + low-rank adaptation
        original_out = self.original(x)
        lora_out = {{diff_adv_new_lora_b3}}
        return original_out + lora_out * self.scale

questions.title: DiT (Diffusion Transformer) Block with AdaLN
questions.description: Implement a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) block that uses adaptive layer normalization (adaLN-Zero) for timestep and class conditioning.
questions.info: DiT (Peebles & Xie, 2023) replaces the U-Net backbone with a Vision Transformer (ViT) for diffusion models. The key architectural innovation is adaLN-Zero: instead of standard LayerNorm, the model predicts scale (gamma) and shift (beta) parameters from the conditioning signal (timestep + class label), and also predicts a per-layer gating parameter alpha initialized to zero. The conditioning is processed as: adaLN(h) = gamma * LayerNorm(h) + beta, followed by gating: alpha * block_output. The zero initialization of alpha ensures the transformer initially acts as an identity function, improving training stability. DiT achieves state-of-the-art FID on ImageNet.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DiTBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden_size, num_heads, cond_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(hidden_size, elementwise_affine=False)
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(hidden_size, num_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(hidden_size, elementwise_affine=False)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden_size, 4 * hidden_size),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(4 * hidden_size, hidden_size)
        )
        # adaLN-Zero: predict 6 modulation parameters
        self.adaLN_modulation = nn.Sequential(
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(cond_dim, {{diff_adv_new_dit_b1}})
        )
        # Initialize the last linear to zero for zero-init gating
        nn.init.zeros_(self.adaLN_modulation[-1].weight)
        nn.init.zeros_(self.adaLN_modulation[-1].bias)
    
    def forward(self, x, c):
        """x: (B, N, D) patch tokens, c: (B, cond_dim) conditioning."""
        # Get modulation parameters
        gamma1, beta1, alpha1, gamma2, beta2, alpha2 = self.adaLN_modulation(c).chunk(6, dim=-1)
        
        # Modulated self-attention
        h = {{diff_adv_new_dit_b2}}
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(h, h, h)
        x = x + alpha1.unsqueeze(1) * attn_out
        
        # Modulated MLP
        h = (1 + gamma2.unsqueeze(1)) * self.norm2(x) + beta2.unsqueeze(1)
        x = x + {{diff_adv_new_dit_b3}} * self.mlp(h)
        return x

Unlike U-Net based models, DiT processes all patches uniformly with full self-attention, making spatial reasoning more flexible. questions.title: Latent Diffusion Model Encode/Decode Pipeline

The diffusion model operates entirely on z, with the VAE encoder and decoder used only at the boundaries. The scaling factor (0.18215 for SD 1.x) normalizes the latent distribution to have unit variance. This two-stage approach enables high-resolution image generation with modest GPU requirements.

To make the super-resolution stages robust to imperfections from the previous stage, conditioning augmentation adds Gaussian noise to the low-res input at a random level during training, and the noise level is provided as an additional conditioning signal. At inference, a small augmentation level (e.g., sigma=0.1) is used. This simple technique dramatically improves cascaded generation quality by preventing error accumulation between stages.

questions.title: CFG Rescale (Addressing Over-Saturation)
questions.description: Implement the CFG rescale technique that prevents color over-saturation caused by high classifier-free guidance scales.
questions.info: High classifier-free guidance scales (w > 7) often produce over-saturated, high-contrast images. Lin et al. (2023, 'Common Diffusion Noise Schedules and Sample Steps are Flawed') proposed a simple fix: after applying CFG, rescale the guided noise prediction to match the standard deviation of the conditional prediction. The rescaled output is: eps_final = phi * eps_rescaled + (1 - phi) * eps_cfg, where eps_rescaled = eps_cfg * (std(eps_cond) / std(eps_cfg)) and phi controls the rescale strength (typically 0.7). This preserves the directional guidance while preventing magnitude explosion that causes saturation.

import torch

def cfg_with_rescale(eps_cond, eps_uncond, guidance_scale, rescale_phi=0.7):

    Args:
        eps_cond: conditional noise prediction
        eps_uncond: unconditional noise prediction
        guidance_scale: w, guidance strength
        rescale_phi: rescale interpolation factor (0 = no rescale, 1 = full rescale)
    Returns:
        eps_final: rescaled guided prediction
    """
    # Standard CFG
    eps_cfg = eps_uncond + guidance_scale * (eps_cond - eps_uncond)
    
    # Compute per-channel std ratio
    std_cond = {{diff_adv_new_cfgr_b1}}
    std_cfg = eps_cfg.std(dim=[2, 3], keepdim=True)

    # Interpolate between rescaled and original CFG
    eps_final = {{diff_adv_new_cfgr_b3}}
    return eps_final

questions.title: Rectified Flow Training and Sampling
questions.description: Implement training and Euler sampling for Rectified Flows (Liu et al., 2022), which learn straight-line transport between noise and data distributions.
questions.info: Rectified Flows (Liu et al., 2022) learn an ODE that transports samples along straight lines between the noise distribution (t=0) and data distribution (t=1). The interpolation is x_t = (1-t)*z + t*x_0, where z ~ N(0,I). The model learns the velocity field v_theta(x_t, t) to match the target velocity x_0 - z. At inference, Euler integration with just a few steps follows the learned flow from noise to data. The key insight is that straight paths are optimal for transport, minimizing the kinetic energy. Rectified flows can be iteratively 'reflow'd to make paths even straighter, enabling fewer sampling steps.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def rectified_flow_loss(velocity_model, x_0):

    Args:
        velocity_model: v_theta(x_t, t)
        x_0: clean data (B, C, H, W)
    """
    B = x_0.shape[0]
    t = torch.rand(B, device=x_0.device)
    z = torch.randn_like(x_0)
    t_bc = t.view(-1, *([1] * (x_0.dim() - 1)))
    
    # Linear interpolation path
    x_t = {{diff_adv_new_rf_b1}}
    target_velocity = x_0 - z
    
    pred_velocity = velocity_model(x_t, t)
    return F.mse_loss(pred_velocity, target_velocity)

@torch.no_grad()
def rectified_flow_sample(velocity_model, shape, device, num_steps=50):

    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)  # start from noise at t=0
    dt = {{diff_adv_new_rf_b2}}
    for i in range(num_steps):
        t = torch.full((shape[0],), i * dt, device=device)
        v = velocity_model(x, t)
        x = {{diff_adv_new_rf_b3}}
    return x

questions.title: SDXL Micro-Conditioning (Size and Crop Conditioning)

questions.info: SDXL (Podell et al., 2023) introduces micro-conditioning to handle the fact that training images have varying resolutions and are often cropped. The model is conditioned on: (1) the original image resolution (height, width), and (2) the crop coordinates (top, left). These are encoded using sinusoidal embeddings and concatenated to the timestep embedding. At inference, setting the original size to the target size and crop coordinates to (0,0) tells the model to generate a full, uncropped image at the desired resolution. This simple trick dramatically reduces common artifacts like cut-off objects and unnatural compositions.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

def fourier_encode(values, dim=256):
    """Sinusoidal encoding for scalar values."""
    half = dim // 2
    freqs = torch.exp(-math.log(10000) * torch.arange(half, device=values.device) / half)
    args = values[:, None] * freqs[None, :]
    return torch.cat([torch.sin(args), torch.cos(args)], dim=-1)

        self.proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim * 6, time_dim)

        Args:

            crop_coords: (B, 2) crop (top, left)
            target_size: (B, 2) target (height, width)
            time_emb: (B, time_dim)
        """

        embeds = [fourier_encode(all_conds[:, i]) for i in range(6)]
        micro_emb = {{diff_adv_new_sdxlmc_b2}}
        projected = self.proj(micro_emb)
        return {{diff_adv_new_sdxlmc_b3}}

questions.title: Self-Conditioning for Diffusion Models
questions.description: Implement the self-conditioning technique where the model's own previous x_0 prediction is fed back as an additional input to improve coherence.
questions.info: Self-conditioning (Chen et al., 2022 'Analog Bits') provides the model with its own previous x_0 estimate as additional context. During training, with 50% probability, a first forward pass predicts x_0 (with stop-gradient), and this prediction is concatenated channel-wise to x_t for a second forward pass. The other 50% of the time, a zero tensor is used (no self-conditioning). At inference, each step uses the x_0 prediction from the previous step as the self-conditioning input. This technique improves sample quality by giving the model a 'rough draft' to refine, and is used in models like Imagen and Recurrent Flow Matching.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def self_conditioning_training_step(model, x_0, t, alpha_bar, p_self_cond=0.5):
    """Training step with self-conditioning.
    model expects input of shape (B, 2*C, H, W): concat of x_t and x0_estimate.
    """
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_0)
    x_t = q_sample(x_0, t, alpha_bar, noise)
    
    # With probability p, compute a self-conditioning estimate
    if torch.rand(1).item() < p_self_cond:
        with torch.no_grad():
            # First pass with zero self-conditioning
            zeros = torch.zeros_like(x_t)
            eps_initial = model(torch.cat([x_t, zeros], dim=1), t)
            x0_estimate = {{diff_adv_new_sc_b1}}
    else:
        x0_estimate = torch.zeros_like(x_0)
    
    # Main forward pass with self-conditioning
    model_input = {{diff_adv_new_sc_b2}}
    eps_pred = model(model_input, t)
    loss = {{diff_adv_new_sc_b3}}
    return loss

questions.title: Min-SNR Weighted Loss for Diffusion Training
questions.description: Implement the Min-SNR-gamma loss weighting strategy that balances the loss across different noise levels for more stable diffusion training.
questions.info: Hang et al. (2023, 'Efficient Diffusion Training via Min-SNR Weighting Strategy') observed that the standard DDPM loss implicitly assigns very different weights to different timesteps, causing conflicting gradients. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at timestep t is SNR(t) = alpha_bar_t / (1 - alpha_bar_t). The Min-SNR-gamma weighting clips the SNR-based weight: weight(t) = min(SNR(t), gamma) / SNR(t), where gamma is typically 5. This reduces the weight for low-noise timesteps (high SNR) that dominate training, leading to faster convergence and better FID scores. It can be applied to any parameterization (noise, x_0, or v).

Min-SNR weight: min(SNR, gamma) / SNR. This clips the weight to at most 1 for high SNR.

questions.title: SDEdit (Image-to-Image with Diffusion)
questions.description: Implement SDEdit (Meng et al., 2021) which performs image-to-image translation by adding noise to a source image and denoising with a text-conditioned model.
questions.info: SDEdit (Meng et al., 2021) enables image-to-image generation without task-specific training. Given a source image, noise is added to a specific timestep t_start (controlled by a 'strength' parameter), and then the standard reverse diffusion process denoises it with optional text conditioning. Lower strength (fewer noising steps) preserves more of the original image structure; higher strength (more noising steps) allows more creative freedom. With strength=1.0, the process starts from pure noise (identical to text-to-image). This technique is widely used in Stable Diffusion's img2img pipeline.

blanks.hint: The starting timestep is strength * T. Higher strength means more noise and more steps.
Forward-diffuse the source image to t_start using the standard q_sample function.
